questions.scenario:
A production apply fails with:

```text
Error: Error acquiring the state lock

Lock Info:
  Operation: OperationTypeApply
  Who: mira@example.com
  Path: dp-terraform-state-prod/devpolaris-orders/prod/terraform.tfstate
```

What should Jamal do first?

options.text: Check whether Mira's apply is still active before attempting any unlock or second apply.
Immediately force-unlock because all locks are stale after an error.
Copy the state file locally and apply without the backend.
Delete the backend block so Terraform falls back to local state.

questions.explanation: A lock during an apply is normal protection. The first step is to verify whether the listed run is still active. Force-unlocking an active apply brings back the race locking was meant to prevent.

questions.scenario: Two CI jobs target the same production state within a minute. The team wants later jobs to wait briefly instead of failing immediately when another apply is finishing. Which behavior is appropriate?

options.text: Use a lock timeout so the second run waits for the lock, while still allowing only one writer at a time.
Disable locking for CI because the jobs are automated.
Run both applies with separate local state files.
Give both jobs write access to the state object so the backend can merge them.

questions.explanation: A lock timeout keeps the one-writer rule but lets queued operations wait for a short, intentional period. It is different from ignoring the lock.

questions.scenario: A lock has been held for four hours. The listed owner confirms their laptop crashed during `terraform apply`, and no CI run is active. What is the safest next step?

options.text: Use the recorded lock ID to force-unlock, then run a plan to verify state and reality still match before applying changes.
Force-unlock every state file in the bucket so future applies are not blocked.
Delete the state object because a crashed apply means state is no longer useful.
Commit a local copy of state so everyone can continue from Git.

questions.explanation: Force-unlock is for a confirmed stale lock. After unlocking, a plan is the low-risk check that Terraform still understands the managed objects before any new write.

questions.scenario: A reviewer is explaining what Terraform state locking does and does not do. Which statements are correct?

options.text: Locking helps prevent two writers from updating the same state record at the same time.
Locking does not prove the proposed infrastructure change is safe or approved.
A stale lock should be removed only after confirming no active run still owns it.
Locking lets a team skip backend access controls because state writes are serialized.

questions.explanation: Locking serializes state writes. It is not a substitute for review, backend access control, state protection, or careful stale-lock cleanup.
